tax: Placed at family rank, Chalk-Kin Family is defined by composition and formation mechanics, not by colour, size, or danger: a calcite body fed by lime-supersaturated water. A new genus belongs here only if it shows a calcite body with its own seep or drip source.
ap: The grouping is recognised by a hollow tufa tower or a flat shimmering crust. A field surveyor confirms membership from at least one persistent marker — a white lime film on surrounding stone — which survives even when the body disperses.
eco: Chalk-Kin Family occupies active tufa terraces and lime-drip caverns. Persistence is elemental rather than reproductive: members form where a calcium-supersaturated seep or drip deposits a calcite nucleus and recur with the seep or drip refills, sustaining themselves on lime supersaturation.
beh: Across the grouping, behaviour is the disturbance-response of the medium: jets or spreads on approach. Roles, moods, and summoning circumstances are recorded on the entries below, not treated as new taxa.
traitsText: Shared characters: calcite body and hydraulic sensing. These are grouping-level traits only; specific resistances, abilities, and field signs belong to each genus and species beneath this node.
abilities: Grouping-level abilities are the broad capacities implied by the shared body: lime-water jetting and crust-sealing. Named, bounded powers are assigned to the species that actually possess them.
bg: In the Throat, Chalk-Kin Family matters because makes lime-seep terraces and drip caverns caustic hazards. It lets survey reports sort calcareous hazards by habit — jet versus spread without collapsing distinct hazards into one vague label.
g: Chalk-Kin Family is the family-rank grouping of calcareous precipitate elementals of lime-seep and drip origin within the True Elementals. Its members share a calcium-carbonate body grown from supersaturated seep or drip, and divide below into genus groups by hollow tube growth versus flat sheet growth. The chalk family makes active lime-water features into hazards.
